:::: pattern-example-title-block
::: pattern-example-title-block-image
![State](../../../../images/patterns/cards/state-mini63fb.png?id=f4018837e0641d1dade756b6678fd4ee){srcset="/images/patterns/cards/state-mini-2x.png?id=7e24398b27a43c7bd286fc0ea54d2a35 2x"}
:::

# **State** を Rust で {#state-を-rust-で .pattern-example-title-block-title}
::::

:::::::::::: pattern-example-body
::: pattern-example-brief
**State** は[、]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}振る舞いに関するデザインパターンの一つで[、]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}オブジェクトの内部状態が変化した時にその振る舞いを変更することを可能とします[。]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}

このパターンは[、]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}状態に関連した振る舞いを個別の状態のクラスへ抽出し[、]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}元のオブジェクトが作業を自分で行わず[、]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}これらのクラスのインスタンスに委任することを強制します[。]{.chpule2}[
]{.chpuri2}
:::

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-bottom: 40px;"}
[ State の詳細 ](../../state.html){.btn .btn-lg .btn-primary}
:::

::::::::: {.sidebar-navigation .with-scroll}
::: en-title
ナビゲーション
:::

::: en-intro
 [はじめに](#)
:::

::: en-intro
 [Music Player](#example-0)
:::

::: en-file
 [player](#example-0--player-rs)
:::

::: en-file
 [state](#example-0--state-rs)
:::

::: en-file
 [main](#example-0--main-rs)
:::
:::::::::

The **State** pattern is related to a finite-state machine (FSM)
concept, however, instead of implementing a lot of conditional
statements, each state is represented by a separate type that implements
a common state trait. Transitions between states depend on the
particular trait implementation for each state type.

The State Pattern in Rust is described in detail in [The Rust
Book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch17-03-oo-design-patterns.html).
::::::::::::

[]{#example-0}

## Music Player {#example-0-title}

Let\'s build a music player with the following state transitions:

<figure class="image">
<img
src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"
data-fetchpriority="high" alt="State machine" />
</figure>

There is a base trait `State` with `play` and `stop` methods which make
state transitions:

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>pub trait State {
    fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt;;
    fn stop(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt;;
}</code></pre>
</figure>

`next` and `prev` don\'t change state, there are default implementations
in a separate `impl dyn State` block that cannot be overridden.

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>impl dyn State {
    pub fn next(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        self
    }

    pub fn prev(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        self
    }
}</code></pre>
</figure>

Every state is a type implementing the `trait State`:

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>pub struct StoppedState;
pub struct PausedState;
pub struct PlayingState;

impl State for StoppedState {
    ...
}

impl State for PausedState {
    ...
}</code></pre>
</figure>

Anyways, it works as follows:

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>let state = Box::new(StoppedState);   // StoppedState.
let state = state.play(&amp;mut player);  // StoppedState -&gt; PlayingState.
let state = state.play(&amp;mut player);  // PlayingState -&gt; PausedState.</code></pre>
</figure>

Here, the same action `play` makes a transition to different states
depending on where it\'s called from:

1.  `StoppedState`\'s implementation of `play` starts playback and
    returns `PlayingState`.

    <figure class="code">
    <pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.play();

        // Stopped -&gt; Playing.
        Box::new(PlayingState)
    }</code></pre>
    </figure>

2.  `PlayingState` pauses playback after hitting the \"play\" button
    again:

    <figure class="code">
    <pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.pause();

        // Playing -&gt; Paused.
        Box::new(PausedState)
    }</code></pre>
    </figure>

The methods are defined with a special `self: Box<Self>` notation.

Why is that?

1.  First, `self` is not a reference, it means that the method is a
    \"one shot\", it consumes `self` and exchanges onto another state
    returning `Box<dyn State>`.
2.  Second, the method consumes the boxed object like `Box<dyn State>`
    and not an object of a concrete type like `PlayingState`, because
    the concrete state is unknown at compile time.

#### []{#example-0--player-rs .anchor} **player.rs**

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>/// A music track.
pub struct Track {
    pub title: String,
    pub duration: u32,
    cursor: u32,
}

impl Track {
    pub fn new(title: &amp;&#39;static str, duration: u32) -&gt; Self {
        Self {
            title: title.into(),
            duration,
            cursor: 0,
        }
    }
}

/// A music player holds a playlist and it can do basic operations over it.
pub struct Player {
    playlist: Vec&lt;Track&gt;,
    current_track: usize,
    _volume: u8,
}

impl Default for Player {
    fn default() -&gt; Self {
        Self {
            playlist: vec![
                Track::new(&quot;Track 1&quot;, 180),
                Track::new(&quot;Track 2&quot;, 165),
                Track::new(&quot;Track 3&quot;, 197),
                Track::new(&quot;Track 4&quot;, 205),
            ],
            current_track: 0,
            _volume: 25,
        }
    }
}

impl Player {
    pub fn next_track(&amp;mut self) {
        self.current_track = (self.current_track + 1) % self.playlist.len();
    }

    pub fn prev_track(&amp;mut self) {
        self.current_track = (self.playlist.len() + self.current_track - 1) % self.playlist.len();
    }

    pub fn play(&amp;mut self) {
        self.track_mut().cursor = 10; // Playback imitation.
    }

    pub fn pause(&amp;mut self) {
        self.track_mut().cursor = 43; // Paused at some moment.
    }

    pub fn rewind(&amp;mut self) {
        self.track_mut().cursor = 0;
    }

    pub fn track(&amp;self) -&gt; &amp;Track {
        &amp;self.playlist[self.current_track]
    }

    fn track_mut(&amp;mut self) -&gt; &amp;mut Track {
        &amp;mut self.playlist[self.current_track]
    }
}</code></pre>
</figure>

#### []{#example-0--state-rs .anchor} **state.rs**

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>use cursive::views::TextView;

use crate::player::Player;

pub struct StoppedState;
pub struct PausedState;
pub struct PlayingState;

/// There is a base `State` trait with methods `play` and `stop` which make
/// state transitions. There are also `next` and `prev` methods in a separate
/// `impl dyn State` block below, those are default implementations
/// that cannot be overridden.
///
/// What is the `self: Box&lt;Self&gt;` notation? We use the state as follows:
/// ```rust
///   let prev_state = Box::new(PlayingState);
///   let next_state = prev_state.play(&amp;mut player);
/// ```
/// A method `play` receives a whole `Box&lt;PlayingState&gt;` object,
/// and not just `PlayingState`. The previous state &quot;disappears&quot; in the method,
/// in turn, it returns a new `Box&lt;PausedState&gt;` state object.
pub trait State {
    fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt;;
    fn stop(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt;;
    fn render(&amp;self, player: &amp;Player, view: &amp;mut TextView);
}

impl State for StoppedState {
    fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.play();

        // Stopped -&gt; Playing.
        Box::new(PlayingState)
    }

    fn stop(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, _: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        // Change no state.
        self
    }

    fn render(&amp;self, _: &amp;Player, view: &amp;mut TextView) {
        view.set_content(&quot;[Stopped] Press &#39;Play&#39;&quot;)
    }
}

impl State for PausedState {
    fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.pause();

        // Paused -&gt; Playing.
        Box::new(PlayingState)
    }

    fn stop(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.pause();
        player.rewind();

        // Paused -&gt; Stopped.
        Box::new(StoppedState)
    }

    fn render(&amp;self, player: &amp;Player, view: &amp;mut TextView) {
        view.set_content(format!(
            &quot;[Paused] {} - {} sec&quot;,
            player.track().title,
            player.track().duration
        ))
    }
}

impl State for PlayingState {
    fn play(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.pause();

        // Playing -&gt; Paused.
        Box::new(PausedState)
    }

    fn stop(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.pause();
        player.rewind();

        // Playing -&gt; Stopped.
        Box::new(StoppedState)
    }

    fn render(&amp;self, player: &amp;Player, view: &amp;mut TextView) {
        view.set_content(format!(
            &quot;[Playing] {} - {} sec&quot;,
            player.track().title,
            player.track().duration
        ))
    }
}

// Default &quot;next&quot; and &quot;prev&quot; implementations for the trait.
impl dyn State {
    pub fn next(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.next_track();

        // Change no state.
        self
    }

    pub fn prev(self: Box&lt;Self&gt;, player: &amp;mut Player) -&gt; Box&lt;dyn State&gt; {
        player.prev_track();

        // Change no state.
        self
    }
}</code></pre>
</figure>

#### []{#example-0--main-rs .anchor} **main.rs**

<figure class="code">
<pre class="code" lang="rust"><code>mod player;
mod state;

use cursive::{
    event::Key,
    view::Nameable,
    views::{Dialog, TextView},
    Cursive,
};
use player::Player;
use state::{State, StoppedState};

// Application context: a music player and a state.
struct PlayerApplication {
    player: Player,
    state: Box&lt;dyn State&gt;,
}

fn main() {
    let mut app = cursive::default();

    app.set_user_data(PlayerApplication {
        player: Player::default(),
        state: Box::new(StoppedState),
    });

    app.add_layer(
        Dialog::around(TextView::new(&quot;Press Play&quot;).with_name(&quot;Player Status&quot;))
            .title(&quot;Music Player&quot;)
            .button(&quot;Play&quot;, |s| execute(s, &quot;Play&quot;))
            .button(&quot;Stop&quot;, |s| execute(s, &quot;Stop&quot;))
            .button(&quot;Prev&quot;, |s| execute(s, &quot;Prev&quot;))
            .button(&quot;Next&quot;, |s| execute(s, &quot;Next&quot;)),
    );

    app.add_global_callback(Key::Esc, |s| s.quit());

    app.run();
}

fn execute(s: &amp;mut Cursive, button: &amp;&#39;static str) {
    let PlayerApplication {
        mut player,
        mut state,
    } = s.take_user_data().unwrap();

    let mut view = s.find_name::&lt;TextView&gt;(&quot;Player Status&quot;).unwrap();

    // Here is how state mechanics work: the previous state
    // executes an action and returns a new state.
    // Each state has all 4 operations but reacts differently.
    state = match button {
        &quot;Play&quot; =&gt; state.play(&amp;mut player),
        &quot;Stop&quot; =&gt; state.stop(&amp;mut player),
        &quot;Prev&quot; =&gt; state.prev(&amp;mut player),
        &quot;Next&quot; =&gt; state.next(&amp;mut player),
        _ =&gt; unreachable!(),
    };

    state.render(&amp;player, &amp;mut view);

    s.set_user_data(PlayerApplication { player, state });
}</code></pre>
</figure>

loading="lazy" alt="Stopped" />

loading="lazy" alt="Playing" />
</figure>
